w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Chapmans Trade is for informal traders and township people in South Africa who sell and buy used items through street markets, Facebook groups, or cash deals. Right now, they have problems like not reaching many customers, getting scammed by fake buyers

/sellers, and trouble delivering goods outside cities, which means they lose money. In the future, they want a simple app that verifies users, handles local payments safely, and makes trading easy while keeping money in South Africa.

About 20% of all workers in South Africa are employed in the informal sector, which employs about 3.3 million people. It brings in R900 billion a year only from township economies, but it is still mostly excluded from the R130 billion e-commerce boom of 2025. Since mobile internet became widely available around 2013, the three main issues
no trust between strangers, payment scams via EFT fraud, and no trustworthy delivery tracking
have remained unresolved for more than 12 years.

Although they initially aided township traders, international platforms such as OLX (which was introduced in South Africa in 2007) and Gumtree ultimately failed them. OLX charges 8–12% commission per sale (R80–120 on R1,000 furniture), its English

only interfaces alienate isiXhosa/isiZulu speakers, and its lax moderation allows fake listings to thrive. With 2.5 million SA users per month, Facebook Marketplace has grown in popularity, but it also exacerbates issues like the lack of buyer
person meetings in dangerous locations that result in hijackings

Every year, 2.5 million township dwellers in Gugulethu (100k+), Mitchells Plain (310k), and Khayelitsha (2.4M people) lose 30–50% of their potential income. For instance, a hairdresser in Khayelitsha who sells an old salon chair on Facebook for R2,000 is defrauded or ghosted three or five times, leaving them with R0 after gas is squandered on no-shows. Each dealer loses between R10,000 and R25,000 annually.
To directly address the trust, reach, and scam

/scams): Build secure login/register system with email verification and password hashing, tested successfully for 50 test users with zero login failures or unauthorized access attempts. This stops fake accounts that cause 30-50% income loss.

/no search): Create product listing form and basic search by category/price/keyword, supporting 100 sample items where users can browse and add to cart without errors. This helps sellers reach more township buyers beyond street markets.
Goal 3 (Fixes bad delivery tracking): Develop simple order system with status updates (pending
end test purchases from listing to order confirmation. This reduces no-show
These goals bring safer trading, wider reach for sellers, and reliable processes, helping informal traders earn more steadily.
Provide detailed information about proposed procedures, if available, and the scope of work. Include information on activities such as training, testing, and actual work required.
Scope: Basic C2C site using HTML/CSS/JavaScript (front), PHP (back), MySQL (database). Features: user login, list/view items, simple cart/orders, basic profile. No payments or chat
focus on core CRUD operations; runs locally on XAMPP.
For the homepage, login/register, product list, single product view, add product form, cart, and user profile, create five to seven wireframes using pen and paper or Figma.

Five tables should be included in the database schema design: users (id, username, email, password_hash, created_at), products (id, seller_id, title, description, price, category, image_path), categories (id, name), cart (session_id, product_id, quantity), and orders (id, buyer_id, product_id, status, order_date).
To test, create SQL CREATE TABLE commands and add 20 example goods.
Create an empty database called chapmans_trade and set up XAMPP.
To test, create SQL CREATE TABLE commands and add 20 example goods.
Create an empty database called chapmans_trade and set up XAMPP.
Use Bootstrap 5 to create responsive HTML pages with a navigation bar, client-side validation (JS) login/registration forms, product listing grid, search form, and cart summary table.
Custom CSS style: mobile-first responsive design (<600px widths), township colours (bright oranges/blues)
Add JavaScript for dynamic cart updates (add/remove products), form validation (email format, price figures), and straightforward AJAX search using get() that doesn't require a page reload.
Building (Weeks 3-6): Code login pages, listing forms, search results, cart display.
Using prepared statements, create config.php with a PDO database connection.

Create the following PHP scripts: products.php (CRUD operations), search.php (LIKE queries), cart.php (session management), orders.php (insert order, clear cart), and login.php (session start, password_verify), register.php (password_hash, email check).
Add product picture file uploads (up to 2 MB, stored in the /uploads/ folder with unique names).
Use input sanitisation, htmlspecialchars(), and PDO to avoid SQL injection as basic security measures.
Link PHP backends to frontend forms using POST requests.
Test the whole user flow: sign up, log in, add products, search, add to cart, and place an order.
Troubleshoot typical problems including session timeouts, unsuccessful picture uploads, and search results that are not showing up.
Include success messages and error pages (404, login failed).
Make 100 sample listings and 50 test accounts by hand.
Test edge cases include faulty photos, duplicate emails, empty searches, and carts with zero items.
Make a two-minute demonstration film that covers every function.
Create a two-page user guide for township traders that includes screenshots.
All files should be packaged in a ZIP file for submission along with a database dump.

Chapmans Trade offers informal merchants a safe, user-friendly internet platform that they may use on low-end cellphones with minimal data, therefore directly addressing South Africa's township selling issues. This student-built solution employs free technology (HTML/CSS/JS/PHP/MySQL) to provide genuine value for 2.5 million prospective users in places like Mitchells Plain and Khayelitsha, in contrast to global platforms that demand exorbitant fees and disregard local requirements.

address the specific issues that were noted, including 30–50% fraud losses, a restricted buyer base, and persistent no-show issues that have lasted for more than ten years. When township merchants are successful, they may offer used clothing, electronics, and furniture to a larger local audience without fear, which might result in a 20–40% increase in their revenue due to improved market access and trust.

From a learning standpoint, this shows mastery of full-stack web development within university constraints: session management for carts, responsive Bootstrap design for mobile-first township users, PDO security against SQL injection, and proper database normalization
all deployable on free XAMPP for instant testing. Steady progress toward a functional prototype prepared for peer testing and lecturer assessment is ensured by the
In the end, Chapmans Trade demonstrates how easily available online technologies may close the R900 billion informal sector gap in South Africa by
street sellers into digital entrepreneurs and developing critical IT skills for practical application.
K. Mercy Makhitha and Ms. Kate Ngobeni (2023). Factors influencing the online clothing shopping intention of emerging township consumers in South Africa: The mediation effect of attitude.
Worx, Mastercard Report Predicts R130 Million Surge in SA’s E-Commerce Sector | African Business Technology News
GROWTH POWERING Insights into the businesses, challenges, and growth path- ways shaping the township informal economy
Stat SA: Department: Statistics South Africa (2025).
